Source: 2022 Quantum Threat Timeline Report, Global Risk Institute
- • There is great uncertainty as to when a quantum computer can break the encryption sys-tems used today. Is it within the next 5 years? Or is it not until 30 years from now? 40 experts have been asked to provide their estimate.
- • Over half of the experts estimate that it is unlikely to happen (less than a 5% chance) within 5 years.
- • On the other hand, over half of them believe that it is very likely to happen (over a 95% chance) within the next 30 years.
A Strengthened Danish Ecosystem
As Danish quantum research is considered to be world-class in a number of areas, Denmark has the potential to be able to create a strong competitive position in the long term. But in order for quantum technology to be a benefit to Danish companies, civil society and security, there is a need to strengthen the Danish quantum ecosystem.
Quantum technology is a relatively new technology where there is currently an international race between countries to be at the forefront of the development and application of quantum technologies. This applies in particular to the US and China, but several European countries are also making large investments in quantum technology, and the quantum agenda is receiving increased attention in the EU and NATO. Thus, a national strategy sends a strong signal to our partners and allies about Denmark's commitment.
At a national level, there are also considerations regarding technological sovereignty, cyber and information security, privacy protection and commercial interests.
There is also considerable focus on attracting quantum talents, investments and new companies. Therefore, we must create an attractive Danish ecosystem for quantum technology that can develop and attract companies, experts and investments. A stronger commitment must further expand the Danish quantum ecosystem and strengthen international relations – also to the benefit of our collaboration partners and our technological sovereignty. At the same time, we must ensure that Danish companies and research institutions are sufficiently resilient to handle the security challenges that quantum technology generates.
Link between Part 1 and Part 2
With the security and defense policy challenges, quantum technology can be of fundamental importance to the future global balance of power and our national and economic security. Therefore, it is crucial that our critical knowledge and technology do not end up in the hands of the wrong people. Among other things, this will require widespread security policy consultacion efforts within the Danish quantum ecosystem, and we must cooperate with like-minded countries to ensure that quantum technology is developed and applied in a responsible manner and for the benefit of a better future.
National Strategy for Quantum Technology in Two Parts
Part 1 of the National Strategy for Quantum Technology, which was published in June 2023, sets the intention for the Danish investments in quantum research and knowledge-based innovation. Research and innovation play a decisive role
in ensuring that Danish society and companies will be able to fully realise the potential of quantum technology in the long run. Furthermore, research and innovation of the very highest quality must strengthen Denmark's international position in the global competition for the development of quantum technology.
With Part 2 of the National Strategy for Quantum Technology, the government is taking the next step. Part 2 focuses on strengthening the emerging Danish quantum technology ecosystem, and supporting the development, commercialisation and application of quantum technology in Denmark for the benefit of society, economy, security and international cooperation. A central part of this task is to ensure that the development takes into account the safety and protection of Danish quantum technology, just as international partnerships must contribute to creating the best possible framework conditions for the development of Danish quantum technology.
CommercializationFinancing Talent EU-leadership Security International cooperation International research and innovation cooperation PART 2 PART 1 Improved access to digital research infrastructure Long term and strategic investments
Our effort does not stop here. Along with the strategy, the government will also establish the National Forum for Quantum Technology, which will be tasked with continuously monitoring the implementation of the initiatives. The Forum will also discuss priorities, challenges and needs in the quantum area, which will play a role in the follow-up of the strategy. This is essential as quantum technology is developing rapidly, and because it is uncertain when major breakthroughs will materialize. Therefore, the government will carry out a midterm evaluation in 2025, which will provide the opportunity to follow up on the strategy and adjust it according to the needs that may arise along the way.
The Danish Ministry of Industry, Business and Financial Affairs is the overall responsible for the implementation of Part 2 of the strategy in close cooperation with the other ministries in the interministerial quantum secretariat, which consists of the Ministry of Industry, Business and Financial Affairs, the Ministry of Higher Education and Science, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Defense and the Ministry of Digital Government and Gender Equality.
# Objective
## Part 1

Denmark aims to have one of the world’s leading quantum research environments and to have the ability to effectively convert research into new, applicable technology.
With the Finance Act, DKK 212 million has been set aside for 2023 for research and innovation in the quantum field. The government's ambition is to maintain this high level for the next four years. Over the period 2023-2027, this translates into an allocation of DKK 1 billion for research and innovation in the field of quantum technology.
With Part 1 of the National Strategy for Quantum Technology, increased momentum has been set for the Danish investment in quantum research. This gives Denmark a unique position to also make the most of the commercial potential within the quantum field.
## Part 2

Denmark must utilize the commercial potential of quantum technology for the benefit of Danish business and Denmark's security through a strengthening of the quantum ecosystem. The Danish strongholds must be used to spearhead the application of quantum technology.
An additional DKK 200 million will be allocated for the period 2024-2027 to strengthen commercialisation, security, and international cooperation in the field of quantum technology.
Part 2 focuses on ensuring that the actors in the quantum technology ecosystem have good opportunities to develop and commercialize quantum technologies. In addition, efforts related to Danish security and international cooperation must be strengthened. To support the ambition, Part 2 must contribute to further strengthening the quantum ecosystem for the development and commercialisation of quantum technology, and to increase Denmark's resilience against the threats and security challenges that may be associated with quantum technologies.

## Priority Action 1: Commercialising Quantum Technology

Denmark's strong position in quantum research must benefit both business and society. Our strong research position must be developed to also become a strong business position, which supports Denmark's long-term competitiveness.
Therefore, the government will actively strengthen the emerging Danish ecosystem for applied quantum technology, so that the Danish research environments and start-ups in the quantum field as well as in the established business community with an interest in and potential for using quantum technologies can become even closer connected.
Quantum technology is still in the early maturity stage, and the companies in the quantum field are mainly spin-outs from universities. In order to speed up the commercialisation of quantum technology, there is a need to improve access to facilities and funding, as well as support the development of and access to the right skills. An important part of the work is to increase awareness of the technology's
potential applications. The Boston Consulting Group estimates that, of the 450-850 billion dollars, which generally applicable and faulttolerant quantum computers can generate in value, around 80 percent is expected to come from end users within e.g. life science and financial service companies. Therefore, there is a need for knowledge sharing and guidance on the possibilities of quantum technology for potential end users, so that more people can benefit from quantum technologies.
Reaping the gains requires flexibility and risk tolerance on the behalf of the companies, but also for employees to have the right skills and knowledge. Denmark has a strong higher education system in quantum science, particularly in physics, chemistry, nanoscience, photonics, mathematics and computer science. But there is a need to secure talent development and meet an increasing need for interdisciplinary knowledge and skills to be successful with quantum technology – at both educational and research institutions and in the business world.
Examples of quantum technology consultants and users
- • Danske Bank and Energinet – participate in field experiments with quantum communication
- • Novo Nordisk – is investigating quantum simulation and computing for drug discovery
- • TDC NET – has used quantum algorithms for route optimization for service technicians
- • KPMG – has located its global quantum hub in Denmark and focuses on applied quantum technology via advisory and consulting services
1
## Quantum House Danmark

As part of the existing education, research and innovation environment around Nørre Campus in Copenhagen, the government in collaboration with a wide circle of stakeholders will initiate Quantum House Denmark, which will strengthen the physical environment within applied quantum technology. The effort must bring together the players within quantum technology in a competitive environment where there is access to office space, test and measurement equipment, and business development guidance while also creating support networks for development and knowledge sharing. In this way, Quantum House Denmark will also support the exchange of knowledge between existing research institutions and stakeholders such as DTU, KU, NATO's Defense Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA), Danish Quantum Community and others, and act as an important physical framework for the development of the Danish quantum industry consisting of start-ups, established
## Establishinf the Quantum Fund

Quantum technology is complex, long-term and expensive to develop. It is a technology in continuous development, where the full potential is still to be realized. Therefore, the development of quantum technology from research to commercial opportunities requires risk-taking and patient capital that can support the transition from research and innovation to commercialisation and application.
As part of the strategy, the government will establish a European quantum fund based in Denmark. The government plans to establish the fund within Denmark's Export and Investment Fund (EIFO) under the Ministry of Industry, Business and Financial Affairs, as EIFO has experience in creating
Activate the Danish strongholds in quantum technology through use cases and demonstration projects For quantum technology to become a commercial success in Denmark, there is a need for end users to realize the potential in using quantum technology's many possibilities. This could be, for example, in the field of health, where quantum sensors can be used for image diagnostics, or within the optimization of energy resources.
The distance between the companies that offer new quantum technology solutions and companies that can profit from using the solutions must be shortened. This must be done by promoting the development of demonstration projects and "use-cases" within strong Danish positions where the potential
companies, etc. The initiative will be an asset for the companies and investors who want to get involved in the quantum environment around Nørre Campus. The establishment of Quantum House Denmark will also support awareness of Denmark's quantum efforts abroad, thereby helping to attract foreign talent and investments to Denmark.
In connection with Quantum House Denmark, the government, in collaboration with the Novo Nordisk Foundation, will establish a national test center which not only caters for research and innovation at the public research institutions, but also takes into account the needs of startups and small companies including, among others, spin-outs from universities. The test center must provide access to advanced equipment that can be used to test the many elements and ideas that originate from e.g. the research environment and business life within quantum technology.

The final fund size will, however, depend on the extent to which financing can be mobilized from private parties. Thereby, the growing Danish ecosystem for quantum technology can contribute to creating a strong innovative position.
The intention is for the quantum fund to support the efforts of Quantum House Denmark and be seen in connection with efforts such as Deep Tech Laboratory - Quantum under the BioInnovation Institute and NATO's Innovation Fund (NIF). To establish the fund, the government will re-prioritise parts of the unused funds from the Restart Fund to the quantum fund.

Efforts are therefore being made to speed up the integration of quantum technology solutions in Danish companies with a particular focus on use-cases and demonstration projects as essential tools to show how quantum technology can be used in practice. The effort will encompass various supporting activities established through a close and dedicated partnership with researchers, quantum start-ups, pertinent businesses, and participants within the quantum ecosystem, including the Danish Quantum Community (DQC).
## More quantum talent in Denmark

Denmark has strong higher education programs with relevance to the quantum field in particular within physics, chemistry, nanoscience, photonics, mathematics and computer science, but with intensified international competition for the best talents to research and develop quantum technology, there is a need to future-proof the talent base in Denmark by developing, attracting and retaining talent and skills.
Therefore, funds will be established in 2024, from which the higher education institutions can receive grants for the development of new educational offers and courses as well as teaching material within quantum science. Through, e.g., transversal subject packages or new continuing/further education courses, the funds must also help support other subject disciplines being brought into play to a greater extent in relation to contributing to the development of quantum technology.
An international summer school is also being established in Denmark for talented students in quantum science. It must both empower Danish students through interaction with international students, support interest in Danish quantum research and open up opportunities in Danish business life for talented international students. In addition, opportunities are being pursued to increase the intake of international students at master's level, including in the quantum science, e.g. physics, chemistry, nanoscience, photonics, mathematics and computer science.
## National Forum on Quantum Technology - continued from Part 1

The quantum sector must be supported by broad cooperation between key stakeholders in order to promote a broadly based national approach through the National Forum for Quantum Technology, which is an initiative under Part 1 of the strategy. The forum will also be able to take up and discuss crosscutting themes such as how the framework conditions for the ecosystem can be improved and commercialisation of research and talent efforts can be further strengthened. The forum will discuss and make recommendations for future efforts.
NATIONAL STRATEGY F OR QUANTUM TECHNOLOGY -PART 2 7
Priority Action 2: Security as a Foundation for Quantum Technological Development
Quantum technology holds many opportunities but is also associated with security challenges and threats. The rapid development and complexity of quantum technologies make it difficult to assess where and when the greatest challenges lie. This applies, for example, to the development of quantum computers and quantum-safe communication solutions, which must protect our critical digital infrastructure and drive the transition to quantum-safe communication.
Knowledge of potential threats and the ability to deal with them is necessary to, among other things, avoid unwanted knowledge transfer, especially to non-like-minded states that can use this knowledge as part of their military capacity building. In addition, the ability to resist decryption of both private companies' and public authorities' sensitive and socially critical data is crucial.
International cooperation, investments and exports are crucial for the development of quantum technologies. But openness also creates vulnerabilities. Investments must be made on a mutually informed basis, just as exports of critical Danish quantum technology must not end up in the wrong places,
where it can be used in conflict with Danish values and interests.
Development, use of and protection against quantum technology in Denmark therefore requires that Danish authorities work to strengthen Denmark's critical digital infrastructure against the potential threats that result from quantum technology. At the same time, efforts in relation to quantum technology protection must be strengthened, so that the quantum ecosystem can build resilience against the unwanted transfer of critical knowledge and technology.
Denmark must be a safe, open and flexible country when it comes to developing quantum technology, where technological development and commer-cialisation is based on a secure foundation in close cooperation with like-minded international partners. It will be important to strike the right balance between regulation, protection, and innovation. Denmark would like to help set the rules of the game, so that regulation can support innovation and commercialisation and, at the same time, protect citizens, companies, and public authorities.

7
8
Strengthen Denmark's critical digital infrastructure against the threats from quantum technologies
The development of certain quantum technologies can threaten Denmark's digital and social security. At present, sensitive and classified data can potentially be in danger of being collected with the purpose of decrypting the data when a sufficiently powerful quantum computer is available. This creates new requirements for how we need to secure Denmark's critical digital infrastructure against the threat from quantum technology.
Denmark must promptly initiate the work to strengthen Denmark's critical digital infrastructure against compromise from, for example, quantum computers.
Increased security in the Danish quantum society
To increase security in the Danish quantum ecosystem, the advisory efforts of the Police Intelligence Service's (PET) will be significantly enhanced. This means that PET will provide more proactive support to the Danish quantum companies and research institutions with the aim of strengthening their ability to be ensure a proper level of security as new quantum technologies are developed.
The strengthening of the advisory will promote the creation of local security organisations in the quantum ecosystem in Denmark and develop a targeted and modern form of knowledge sharing between the security authorities and the quantum community in Denmark. This will be done through the
Effective regulation and protection of quantum technology
An important framework condition for the development of quantum technology in Denmark is effective regulation and protection of those parts of quantum technology where the risk of misuse is greatest if the technologies end up in the wrong place. Here, it is important that we, from the Danish side, are part of a community that is aligned with our values. In addition to the security consequences, it will be harmful to Danish growth and exports if, for example, Danish quantum technology is linked to military use in non-like-minded countries.
Therefore, some quantum technologies are already subject to regulation from Danish authorities. Among other things, export controls have been placed on certain quantum technologies, and
A secretariat is therefore being established to coordinate the response to this security challenge with the support of an expert group. The secretariat must prepare guidelines and recommendations for handling the challenges as well as a plan for future-proofing Denmark's critical digital infrastructure against the threats that may arise from quantum technologies. The secretariat must also be able to provide guidance on the significance of the quantum threat for the critical digital infrastructure and cyber security, as well as enter collaborations with, for example, companies and research institutions that can help counter the quantum threat.
recently established Quantum Technology Security Forum and by increasing support for building local security organisations, advice on investments and international collaborations, as well as dissemination of an updated threat picture against Danish quantum research.
The initiatives will contribute to security becoming a strategic priority among Danish developers of quantum technology and increase the understanding that security policy is a responsibility that is largely managed in the decisions taken by companies and research institutions.
foreign investments in Danish quantum companies are currently screened by the authorities.
It needs to be easy for Danish companies to get guidance from the authorities. Companies must be able to find out whether the technology they are working with is controlled. Application and case processing must be as flexible and efficient as possible, so that transparency and predictability are achieved for the benefit of investments and exports.
To address this, there will be initiated an effort to strengthen the analysis and management capacity in relation to risks within export control and investment screening and a consequently strengthened guidance effort towards companies and research institutions.
## Priority Action 3:
# Advancing Danish Quantum Interests Internationally

The development of quantum technology is characterized by great international and geopolitical competition. Quantum technology is central to the technological race, given that the first countries to develop and use the technology may face great economic and security advantages. Quantum technology is a new and disruptive technology that contains risks and dilemmas, but is not yet globally regulated as the technology is still under development. There is a need to incorporate international principles, rules and standards for the responsible development and use of quantum technology that are aligned with Danish and European values. Frameworks that can promote the Danish quantum ecosystem.
International cooperation in the EU and with like-minded countries and allies will be decisive for the commercialisation of quantum technology in Denmark. International cooperation must support Denmark's ability to attract talent and companies. This should give Denmark a central position in the global value chains and in the European quantum technology infrastructure. The Danish quantum ecosystem must become a central part of Europe's technological sovereignty.
Denmark must engage actively on the international stage. This applies particularly in the EU, where the European rules are established and from which global norms and standards are shaped. Strategic partnerships with strong and like-minded quantum nations, where we can promote the strong Danish positions, will also be a priority. It will be through international commitment that Denmark can work to ensure that quantum technology's great potential is utilized solve global challenges. Through new breakthroughs in research and innovation, we must jointly contribute to a better future.
Denmark is in a unique position when it comes to taking on that task. Standing on the shoulders of Niels Bohr's pioneering research and a contemporary research environment internationally. Denmark has a special voice in the international quantum ecosystem. It must be used to leave a Danish mark on the global rules and accelerate the development, growth, and export of Danish quantum technology. If the Danish quantum ecosystem is to continue to be world-leading, it also requires an intensified effort to attract foreign companies that can supply the knowledge, talent and capital needed to develop the quantum technologies of the future.

Denmark in a leading role in the development of quantum technology in Europe
Denmark cannot fully realize the potential of quantum technology without close and committed cooperation with our neighboring European countries. Increased cooperation must support knowledge sharing and the scaling of quantum products and services to a level where we in Europe can compete internationally and thus exert influence on the global development of the technology. This calls for solutions across borders in Europe, and thus the EU's strategic approach and level of ambition will play a decisive role in the years ahead.
International quantum hub will strengthen Denmark's strategic quantum partnerships Denmark must develop international partnerships that can advance the Danish strongholds and promote strategic initiatives with other countries. Since quantum technology has also become geopolitics and security politics, there will be a need for a strengthened diplomatic effort to strike the balance between protection, competition, and international cooperation.
To address this, the government will establish an international quantum hub, which will promote Denmark strongly internationally and ensure progress in new cooperation agreements. The hub will strengthen Denmark's strategic quantum cooperation bilaterally, multilaterally and regionally in the EU and the Nordic region, and with central research institutions and companies in the field of quantum technology. It must be the primary entrance for international actors to the Danish authorities and ecosystem.
A strategic approach to international quantum partnerships will require that partnerships are assessed based on Danish interests and, in certain cases, that a political framework is developed for this purpose. The partnerships may include activities such as coordination of national efforts, regulation,
Danish footprint in international standardisation in the quantum field
The government will strengthen the Danish commitment to the quantum agenda in the European standardisation system by working to ensure that Denmark is given responsibility for a European quantum secretariat that can have a significant influence on the work with the development of standards within quantum technology at European level. In addition, a targeted effort is launched to build a bridge between researchers, government
By virtue of its strong position in quantum research, Denmark has the opportunity to take a leading role in the EU cooperation. In turn, a targeted effort is being launched to strengthen Denmark's commitment to EU initiatives within the development, com-mercialisation, and application of quantum technology. The effort must safeguard secure Danish interests in the development of an internal market for quantum technologies, where supportive regulation, reduction of burdens and barriers as well as access to financing, infrastructure and skilled labor are expected to be central.
research, talent development, public-private partnerships on use-case development or joint investments in infrastructure as well as demonstration and test facilities. The bilateral quantum declaration between the USA and Denmark from 2022 is an example of this, just as cooperation on quantum technology is an important element in the declaration on cooperation between Denmark and Great Britain from 2023.
With the quantum hub, Denmark can assume a central role that is in demand among international partner countries for the benefit of the Danish ecosystem and Denmark's security. In relation to this, Denmark must take advantage of its strongholds to set the tone when it comes to international cooperation on security policy perspectives on quantum technology, including in the EU and NATO cooperation. As part of the initiative, investments are also being made in a strengthened international presence to promote the Danish quantum ecosystem by attracting foreign companies that can contribute with knowledge, talent and capital. At the same time, Denmark’s strengthened international presence must help to support the quantum hub's work in developing bilateral partnerships with countries where there is a strategic and political interest in international cooperation in the quantum field. authorities, innovation projects and standardization bodies to support the development and dissemination of relevant standards, as well as a strengthened effort for knowledge sharing and networking in the quantum area with a focus on the importance of standards for the development of quantum technology in Denmark. The work must contribute to a Danish strategic prioritisation of standardisation activities for the EU's standardisation work program 2024.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Quantum technologies have arrived to mark a turning point in the history of science. Their development will bring about an unprecedented revolution in research and industry because quantum technologies will transform our understanding of the world and our ability to face the great challenges of the 21st century. These are challenges for which Europe must be prepared and for which Spain has transformative proposals.
The digital transformation, driven by the Spanish Government, must take advantage of the historic opportunity presented by quantum technologies to strengthen our digital sovereignty, stimulate our competitiveness and ensure sustainable, ethical and regulated development.
Hence the importance of Spain's Quantum Technologies Strategy 2025-2030. This strategy is the result of consensus and collaboration between two cross-cutting ministries: the Ministry for Digital Transformation and Public Administration and the Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities. But it also stems from a participatory process involving other ministries, universities and research centres, the autonomous communities and local authorities involved in clusters and complementary plans, as well as cooperation with companies, start-ups and the Spanish quantum ecosystem.
This collective effort makes even more sense in the current context, in which global competition for quantum leadership is rapidly intensifying, as is already the case with other disruptive technologies such as Artificial Intelligence. All of this is fully in line with European initiatives and with a keen eye on the international context.
With this Strategy, the Spanish Government is taking on an unprecedented challenge: we are talking about a roadmap that will consolidate all the advances in this field, accelerate the pace of technological progress and amplify its impact on Spain today and tomorrow.
The purpose of this Strategy is to provide a common framework to guide decisions, align resources and build confidence throughout the national quantum ecosystem.
The Strategy also constitutes a call for cooperation, dialogue and coordinated action. We aspire not only to drive scientific and industrial progress, but also to create a robust, integrated and responsible ecosystem that ensures that the benefits of quantum technologies reach all citizens in an inclusive and equitable manner. Public-private collaboration and the active involvement of civil society will be essential to successfully address the ethical, social, regulatory and geopolitical challenges posed by these technologies.
Furthermore, we want tangible results. We want to focus innovation on specific use cases that respond to people's needs and the country's economic and strategic challenges. Quantum technologies open up transformative opportunities in key areas such as new materials, health, logistics, energy, defence, the space sector and digital security. These are areas where their application can make a decisive difference.
Technological innovation requires vision, ambition and commitment. Spain can and must drive a profound, sustainable and inclusive digital transformation.
That is why we invite all players in the sector to join us on the road to quantum Spain. We were late to other revolutions, but in this one we are determined to position ourselves differently from the outset and with determination.
Diana Morant Ripoll,
Minister of Science, Innovation and Universities
Óscar López Águeda, Minister for Digital Transformation and the Civil Service

text: # C C A A P P ÍÍT T U U L L O O I I. .CHAPTER I.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: # Introduction

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Quantum technologies are based on the principles of quantum mechanics, the branch of physics that studies the behaviour of matter and energy at extremely small scales, such as atoms and subatomic particles.
The discoveries of the first quantum revolution in the early 20th century marked a milestone in the understanding and application of the properties of quantum mechanics, such as interactions between molecules, atoms or smaller particles such as photons or electrons, giving rise to technological innovations that we live with today. These have facilitated a momentous advance in communications and computing, as in the case of semiconductors, which are the basis of microprocessors, or lasers, which have played a fundamental role in fibre optic communications.
The second quantum revolution, in which we are currently immersed, goes further and exploits the knowledge we have acquired in handling quantum phenomena. Unlike classical technology, which is governed by the laws of traditional physics, quantum technologies take advantage of quantum phenomena such as superposition, entanglement and interference, which manifest themselves especially in subatomic particles and in solid-state systems at extremely low temperatures, to develop devices with revolutionary capabilities.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## In the next decade, quantum technologies will have a disruptive impact on the economy and society.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Quantum technologies are usually classified into three main groups: quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing and metrology.
Firstly, quantum computing promises exponentially greater computing power than that offered by current classical computing, which will enable us to find more efficient solutions to complex problems, and even to problems that have been unsolvable until now. While a conventional computer operates on bits (each of which takes a defined value, 0 or 1), a quantum computer operates on qubits (physical systems that can be in quantum superpositions of the states 0 and 1). This feature, together with quantum parallelism and interference, greatly increases computing power, reducing calculations that would take centuries or would be completely unfeasible with ordinary computing to a matter of minutes.
While progress is being made towards achieving a universal quantum computer, work is also being done on alternative computational models: quantum simulation and emulation, and quantum-inspired computing. Quantum simulation, also known as analogue quantum computing, solves highly complex problems by modelling their behaviour with a specific quantum system. Quantum emulation, on the other hand, focuses on approximating the behaviour of quantum computers and simulators using specialised algorithms that can be run on classical supercomputers.
Finally, quantum-inspired computing proposes new classical solutions discovered using emulation tools and an understanding of how quantum computer algorithms work.
10 / SPAIN'S NATIONAL QUANTUM TECHNOLOGIES STRATEGY
This capability is expected to pose a risk to digital security, as quantum computers may be able to break current cryptographic algorithms. To mitigate this contingency, post-quantum cryptography (PQC) algorithms, which are resistant to possible attacks from quantum computers, may be implemented.
On the other hand, quantum communication will enable information to be transmitted over long distances, which will improve security and use entanglement and quantum teleportation to extend the distances between the transmitter and receiver. To ensure security, tools such as entanglement and the no-cloning principle will also be used to implement quantum key distribution (QKD) between different points, making it resistant to quantum and classical attacks. In the future, this discipline will possibly allow us to create a quantum network or internet. These networks will open up new, as yet unexplored forms of distributed computing and sensing, as well as enabling us to study the ultimate limits of quantum physics.
Finally, quantum sensors are capable of measuring physical properties with significantly greater precision than traditional sensors. For example, they can detect minimal differences in temperature or changes in the electromagnetic field or pressure with much greater accuracy than is currently possible. Quantum metrology, for its part, uses quantum mechanics to improve the traceability of measurements within the International System of Units and to ensure that they are comparable, reproducible and robust.
Over the next decade, quantum technologies will have a disruptive impact on the economy and, as has happened in the past with other technological revolutions, will transform society in ways that are unimaginable today. It will therefore be essential to master them in order to guarantee the security of communications, data and critical infrastructure, but above all to increase our industrial and economic competitiveness and enable us to operate in increasingly globalised markets.

text: ## r economic impact

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Quantum technologies are emerging as a key driver for the economy and employment in sectors such as cybersecurity, logistics, biotechnology, telecommunications, defence and energy. The global market for quantum technologies is estimated to reach between $106 billion and $173 billion by 2040(1) .
From an economic standpoint, quantum computing could improve efficiency in the financial sector, optimise risk models and accelerate decision-making in complex markets. It is estimated that, on its own, it will have a global market size of between $28 billion and $72 billion by 2035 and between $45 billion and $131 billion by 2040. As for the quantum communication sector, it will reach a market size of between $11 billion and $15 billion by 2035, with values of up to $36 billion in 2040. Meanwhile, quantum sensing, despite its smaller market volume, is estimated to reach a size of $500 million to $2.7 billion by 2035, increasing to $6 billion by 2040.
According to projections, due to the growth in the value of the quantum market and its crosscutting nature, the quantum technology market could generate added value for the economy as a whole of up to $2 trillion by 2035, with this added value coming mainly from its impact on sectors such as chemistry, life sciences, finance and mobility.
The global market size could reach
$173 billion
by 2040
USE CASE
Credit valuation adjustments (Credit adjustments)
valuation
The calculation of credit valuation adjustments (CVA), crucial in financial risk management, can benefit greatly from quantum computing.
Algorithms such as quantum amplitude estimation (QAE) and Monte Carlo simulations enable faster and more accurate assessment of default risks by exploring multiple scenarios simultaneously. This reduces computing costs and speeds up decision-making in volatile markets.
Their ability to model complex scenarios improves risk management in financial institutions, transforming the way uncertainty in markets is assessed.
The development of these technologies will also generate a multiplier effect on the economy by stimulating innovation, attracting foreign investment and encouraging the creation of new companies and start-ups. Currently, the sector has accumulated £42 billion in government funding worldwide and £8.5 billion in quantum start-up investment2. Despite a 27% decline in investment in quantum start-ups in 2023 in favour of generative AI start-ups, quantum technologies continue to attract large investment flows due to their disruptive potential in multiple industries, and their combination with AI will continue to grow in the coming years.
In Europe, the quantum computing market is expected to reach a value of $2.406 billion in 2030, up from $220.7 million in 2022, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 34.8% during the period 2023-2030. This growth is mainly driven by increased public and private investment, as well as growing demand for optimisation solutions in sectors such as
automotive, energy, healthcare and finance. At the business level, there has been a gradual increase in the adoption of quantum computing as a tool for solving complex problems that exceed the capabilities of classical systems, consolidating Europe as a major player in the global race for these emerging technologies.
In addition, quantum technologies are set to profoundly transform the defence and security sector through disruptive capabilities that will radically change electronic warfare and communications, critical infrastructure security, and military intelligence. This will force states to rethink their defence strategies in the face of a rapidly evolving technological landscape.
Key applications such as secure communications systems, platform detection and real-time data accuracy could be significantly improved thanks to the development and deployment of these technologies. Quantum computing will make it possible to compromise, or even break, certain traditional encryption schemes, and drive the development of new methods of attack-resistant quantum cryptography. Quantum communication will also provide communication channels that are virtually invulnerable to interception, strengthening the security of military and government networks. In turn, quantum detection and radar will revolutionise surveillance and reconnaissance capabilities, enabling the detection of stealth aircraft and submarines with unprecedented accuracy.
Finally, quantum sensors will enable ultra-precise navigation without relying on the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signal, a crucial aspect in environments subject to electronic interference or cyberattacks. On the other hand, the availability of unique platforms and restricted spaces free from external interference offers significant potential for the validation and experimentation of quantum sensors. These capabilities can not only strengthen the military sphere, but also have the potential to drive innovations in the civil sector based on this advanced technology.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## r social impact

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: From a social perspective, these technologies have the potential to improve multiple aspects of society, from health to mobility and cybersecurity. In health, quantum computing will enable advanced simulations of biomolecular processes, thereby accelerating the discovery of drugs and personalised treatments, with a projected impact of hundreds of billions of pounds on the pharmaceutical industry5.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Quantum technologies will be a key tool for the security or telecommunications sector, but also for health, mobility, or climate disasters.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
USE CASE
Urban traffic and public transport management can benefit from advances in quantum computing.
Bus route optimisation
By analysing variables such as traffic flows, urban events and mobility patterns in real time, quantum algorithms optimise bus routes, reducing waiting times and improving operational efficiency. This contributes to more agile mobility, reduces congestion and facilitates the adoption of low-emission vehicles.
In transport and logistics, quantum algorithms will be able to optimise routes and reduce congestion, improving the efficiency of public transport and reducing CO₂ emissions.
However, although quantum technology represents innovative solutions that will help resolve the challenges faced by the Ons Hsalǐh approach, unequal access to these technologies could exacerbate economic and technological inequalities. These imbalances would widen the digital and technological divide, limiting access to their benefits in regions with less technological development.
In addition, automation driven by quantum computing could displace some jobs7, requiring retraining strategies to mitigate its impact on the workforce. The lack of skilled talent is also a challenge, as demand for professionals specialising in quantum technologies is expected to increase, which could lead to a shortage of experts in the short term.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## nvironmental impact

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
From an environmental perspective, these technologies can contribute8 significantly to sustainability and the fight against climate change.
Quantum computing has the potential to improve energy production forecasting, enabling more efficient integration of renewable energies into the electricity grid and reducing dependence on fossil fuels. In industry, this technology will accelerate the development of advanced biofuels and new materials, promoting more sustainable alternatives for energy production and storage. It is also expected to play a crucial role in the battery revolution, optimising materials to improve their efficiency and lifespan.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Quantum technologies can contribute to the green transition, for example by improving energy production forecasting.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
USE CASE
The efficient integration of renewable energies depends on accurate forecasting of their production.
Energy production forecasting
Advanced quantum algorithms can analyse large-scale meteorological and operational data to anticipate energy generation, reducing uncertainty and optimising the balance between supply and demand. This allows for better planning in electricity grids and less dependence on fossil fuels, promoting the sustainability of the energy system.
Another key advance is the application of quantum computing in AI, where quantum-inspired algorithms can contribute significantly to energy savings. It is estimated that reducing the parameterisation time of AI models will optimise electricity consumption in data centres, increasing their efficiency and reducing their environmental footprint. This is particularly relevant, as it is projected that up to 15% of global energy will be used for the development of AI models in the coming years9.
Quantum sensors will soon lead a technological revolution in fields as varied as biomedicine, with ultra-precise sensors that will enable the imaging of electromagnetic fields at the cellular level or inertial navigation without GNSS. Applications are also expected in other fields, such as the environment, optimising the distribution of renewable energy and facilitating a more efficient energy transition. Quantum gravimeters are expected to play a crucial role, among other things, in geothermal exploration without the need for invasive drilling and in groundwater monitoring to improve water resource management.
However, the deployment of these technologies also presents environmental challenges. Quantum data centres could consume large amounts of energy and, although they could improve energy efficiency in the long term, in the initial phase they could increase electricity demand, raising the global carbon footprint. Likewise, the deployment of these technologies, such as quantum hardware, could involve industrial processes that are intensive in the search for, extraction and use of natural resources. It is estimated that quantum infrastructure, in its initial phase, could require a significant amount of rare and superconducting materials, posing additional challenges in terms of sustainability and supply chain.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Impact on cy bersecurity and privacy

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
The deployment of these technologies will also entail risks for individuals and their digital rights, and for cybersecurity in general. Quantum computing, for example, could compromise current encryption schemes, exposing vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure and government, financial and commercial structures to potential attacks. This could compromise citizens' privacy and the security of digital systems, creating a crisis of confidence in the protection of personal data, as well as non-personal data such as trade secrets and intellectual property.
r international effort Given the economic, social and environmental impact of these technologies, as well as their
strategic relevance in areas such as defence and security, the major economic powers have designed and are implementing strategies focused on quantum development and deployment, through significant investments with a long-term focus. These economic powers recognise that quantum technologies will be strategic for their competitiveness and, above all, for their technological sovereignty.
Countries such as China stand out, with an estimated $15.3 billion in public investment between 2021 and 2027, focused on security, defence and artificial intelligence, according to announcements made in 2023.
The United States, through initiatives such as the National Quantum Initiative Act 2018 and the CHIPS and Science Act 2022, has structured its support for quantum research with a cumulative public investment of $3.75 billion, to which is added $3.8 billion in private investment, reflecting the strong role of venture capital in its ecosystem.
Canada, for its part, has committed to allocating more than $1.4 billion in public investment between 2012 and 2029, and approximately $1.3 billion in private investment. Its National Quantum Strategy, published in January 2023, prioritises scientific research, attracting talent and commercialising solutions, relying on innovation centres such as DistriQ in Quebec.
Singapore has allocated £138 million to its quantum programme since 2018, with a specific focus on cybersecurity, quantum communication networks and technological interoperability.
On the European continent, Germany announced in 2023 a total investment of €5.2 billion until 2026, with the aim of promoting regional clusters of excellence and developing a universal quantum computer with 100 operational qubits by that year. The United Kingdom, through its 2023 National Quantum Strategy, has committed the equivalent of $4.3 billion in public investment with concrete plans until 2035 for industrial applications and advanced networks. France announced in 2021 a €1.8 billion plan focused on quantum computing, photonics and job creation in the sector. Finally, the Netherlands, through the Quantum Delta NL programme, has allocated €965 million until 2030, with the aim of consolidating its quantum ecosystem as one of the most advanced on the continent11.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## The major economic powers have designed strategies focused on the development and deployment of these technologies, with longterm investments.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
In this context, global competition in quantum technologies is intensifying, and countries are seeking to position themselves politically and economically, while also exploring integration with AI and supercomputing.
In the European Union (EU), reports by Mario Draghi12, Sauli Niinistö13 and Enrico Letta14 have led to a refocusing of our joint action, guiding us towards strengthening the internal market, competitiveness and innovation in dual technologies. In the Competitiveness Compass15 , published in 2025, the Commission recognises that quantum technologies will be fundamental to the EU's economic security and technological sovereignty in the coming decades. In fact, the European Commission itself already set out in the Digital Compass16 in 2021 the goal of developing a quantum computer by 2030.
Many of the national strategies were launched thanks to the momentum generated by the Quantum Technologies Flagship initiative17 , launched in 2017 and initially endowed with €1 billion until 2027. To date, the EU has planned an investment of around €8 billion. Complementing this, the future proposal for a Quantum Act aims to consolidate a pan-European industrial and scientific ecosystem, increasing our economic security and digital autonomy. However,
The fragmentation of national efforts remains a challenge. The EU has therefore urged Member States to strengthen cross-border cooperation and avoid internal competition. This request is reflected in the European Declaration on Quantum Technologies18 , promoted by Spain during its Presidency of the Council of the EU, which prioritised efforts between Member States and the search for consensus. For all these reasons, a common, coordinated and strategic plan is particularly important in order to avoid internal competition between states and duplication of efforts. This plan will take shape through the European Strategy for Quantum Technologies, scheduled for 2025. Spain is present in the European Commission's working groups, participating in the co-creation process of this European initiative from the outset, providing valuable information and positioning on our strengths.
On the other hand, creating synergies and taking advantage of the European scale is key to the development of quantum infrastructures. Priority actions that will enable us to jointly have a network of infrastructures are:
- The EuroQCI19 (European Quantum Communication Infrastructure), for the implementation of a secure quantum communications network, funded by the Cybersecurity pillar of the Digital Europe Programme; and
- EuroQCS1720(Quantum Computing and Simulation Infrastructure) funded by the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking21Digital Europe Programme to create a high-performance supercomputing ecosystem in the EU, integrating networked quantum computers and simulators to strengthen the computing infrastructure.
Likewise, in the field of quantum hardware, the promotion of specific European funding is key to the development of pilot lines of quantum chips, which may cover technologies such as photonics, superconductors, cold atoms, trapped ions, semiconductors and diamonds, taking into account the necessary diversification in these enabling technologies.
In turn, funding programmes such as the STEP Platform22 (Strategic Technologies for Europe Platform), which focuses on projects for the development or manufacture of strategic technologies and can increase funding for them from Member States, the European Tech Champions Initiative (ETCI), to which Spain contributes €700 million23 to finance the latest rounds of venture capital investment, and the European Innovation Council (EIC) Accelerator24 , which focuses on pre-seed and seed operations, will be key to enabling European quantum start-ups to compete globally without relying on foreign capital, as well as accelerating the industrialisation of quantum technologies, safeguarding our strategic autonomy.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: # A common, coordinated and strategic plan among EU Member States is particularly important.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Spain must operate in a highly competitive environment and prioritise its investments in quantum technologies as key to digital sovereignty and economic security.
At the transatlantic level, NATO has activated a strategy25 to address the changes that these technologies are expected to bring, considering them to be emerging and disruptive with the potential to revolutionise the way it operates and conducts its defence operations. Noteworthy examples include the Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA), the Transatlantic Quantum Community (TQC), focused on the application of quantum technologies in defence, and the Science and Technology Organisation26 (STO), which promotes the exchange of information between key players and has recently created a specific Science and Technology Panel (TSI) to cover quantum technologies, among others.
This analysis shows that Spain must operate in a highly competitive environment and prioritise its investments in quantum technologies as a key element for technological sovereignty and national and European economic security. In this scenario, the country cannot afford to fall behind; it must take advantage of the opportunities arising from the development and deployment of these technologies, ensuring their integration into the global value chain and actively contributing to the European quantum ecosystem.
To this end, it is essential to understand Spain's starting point and its ecosystem, so that areas where strengths already exist can be reinforced and leadership opportunities can be developed to the fullest, in addition to preparing society for the disruptive change that these technologies entail, promoting a structured transition that guarantees long-term economic and social benefits. National coordination between key players will be crucial to consolidating a robust and competitive quantum ecosystem that positions Spain as a European leader in this technological revolution and, at the same time, allows it to take advantage of European synergies and resources in the medium and long term.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## USE CASE

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
In the face of extreme weather events, the ability to anticipate risks is essential.
Climate risk simulation
Quantum algorithms can simulate hurricane, drought or flood scenarios with greater accuracy, helping governments and businesses to design more effective mitigation strategies. This strengthens the resilience of infrastructure and economies in the face of climate change.
27

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Practical application of quantum technologies to the SDGs

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
In agriculture (SDG 2: Zero Hunger), quantum computing could optimise crop production, improve resource allocation and facilitate the monitoring of plant health. It would also accelerate the development of new-generation fertilisers and environmentally friendly and safe alternatives to pesticides, speeding up genetic analysis of crops and agricultural improvement processes.
In water management (SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation), technologies such as quantum gravimeters would enable more accurate monitoring of underground aquifers, which are essential for drinking water supply and agriculture. Highly sensitive quantum sensors could detect contaminants in water more quickly and accurately than current technologies, while quantum simulations would contribute to the development of new filtration and purification systems.
For more sustainable cities (SDG 11: Sustainable Cities and Communities), quantum computing would help improve urban planning, optimise traffic and reduce pollution through efficient transport management. It would also facilitate the creation of new materials for more resistant and sustainable infrastructure. It is also expected to contribute to more accurate predictions of extreme weather events such as droughts, earthquakes and floods.
In terms of climate action (SDG 13: Climate Action), quantum computing could accelerate decarbonisation by optimising energy use and reducing CO₂ emissions. Quantum simulations in chemistry and materials science would be key to developing new renewable energy technologies, improving batteries, and advancing carbon removal methods. They would also enable better forecasting of wind and solar energy production, supporting the transition to a sustainable energy system.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: # C C A A P P ÍÍT T U U L L O O I I I I I I. CHAPTER III. .

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Spain: where we stand with quantum technologies

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Spain is starting from a solid position in the development of quantum technologies, with investments made since 2020 thanks to the Recovery, Transformation and Resilience Plan by the Spanish Government, and also thanks to the Autonomous Communities and Local Entities. This has led to significant advances in the development of quantum technologies, especially in quantum computing and communications, as well as the gradual emergence of regional and national ecosystems.
In the scientific field, Spain has a solid and recognised research base in key areas of quantum technologies, as well as growing investment in human capital. The Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities (MICIU) has promoted various initiatives in this field, within the framework of the Spanish Strategy for Science, Technology and Innovation (EECTI)27 2021-2027, and the State Plan for Scientific and Technical Research and Innovation (PEICTI)28 2024-2027.
Spain regularly contributes to the funding of fundamental and applied research groups, which has consolidated its position as a benchmark in various technologies, particularly in the creation of quantum optical communication devices, their integration into commercial networks and in the field of quantum sensors. Since 2016, through the State Research Agency (AEI), a total of 366 projects have been funded with €46.8 million, in addition to €51 million in grants for the recruitment of personnel specialised in quantum technologies (138 Ramón y Cajal research contracts, 122 Juan de la Cierva contracts, 19 Torres Quevedo Programme contracts and 6 grants for doctoral training in collaboration with industry).
35
laboratories collaborate in the Interdisciplinary Thematic Platform on Quantum Technologies of the CSIC
In the field of research, the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC) leads the Interdisciplinary Thematic Platform on Quantum Technologies (PTI QTEP-CSIC)29 , which brings together 35 laboratories from 21 Spanish centres and is organised into three areas: acquisition tasks (sensor technology and metrology), communication, and quantum information processing (computing and simulation). This collaborative network integrates numerous universities, public research bodies and companies into its research and training activities. For example, a quantum computing laboratory has been created in Oviedo (CINN)30 , a quantum communication laboratory in Madrid (ITEFI-CSIC)31 and the inter-university master's degree in quantum technologies in Spain32 , coordinated by the CSIC through the Menéndez Pelayo International University (UIMP), in collaboration with nine other universities.
The Institute of Photonic Sciences (ICFO) has established itself as one of the leading international centres for quantum technologies, spearheading pioneering research in quantum photonics, high-precision quantum sensors and secure quantum communications. Its work has been key in the development of advanced quantum detection systems and in the deployment of infrastructures for quantum key distribution networks.
Another key organisation in Spanish research is the Canary Islands Institute of Astrophysics (IAC), which investigates ways of compensating for the adverse effects of atmospheric turbulence, a key challenge for the implementation of these technologies in operational environments. The IAC is a world leader in the astrophysical application of these techniques.
It is currently building the IAClink infrastructure, a facility that is unique in the world thanks to the direct line of sight between the Roque de los Muchachos and Teide observatories. Interestingly, the first demonstration33 of free-space quantum communications was carried out between the islands of La Palma and Tenerife in 2007.
To promote leadership in fundamental and applied research, it has also been necessary to develop key infrastructure that facilitates the work of researchers. MICIU funds and coordinates three Unique Scientific and Technical Infrastructures (ICTS)34 , which are of considerable importance for quantum technologies:
- Micronanofabs35, composed of three nodes36, is dedicated to micro- and nanofabrication and photonics.
- The Spanish Supercomputing Network (RES)37, coordinated by the Barcelona Supercomputing Centre – National Supercomputing Centre38 , which provides services throughout Spain with supercomputing and quantum computing capabilities and invests in these areas.
- RedIRIS 39, managed by the public entity Red.es, as a national academic communications network and platform for experimentation in these technologies.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## £22 million invested in Quantum Spain; 100% European technology

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
It is worth mentioning the National Deep Tech Strategy, which is currently being developed and promoted by several Spanish government ministries.
In addition, the MICIU, in collaboration with six Autonomous Communities(40) ,has deployed the Complementary Quantum Communication Plan (POCC)41 , with a total investment of €75 million between 2021 and 2025, to support the development of a national-scale infrastructure that will enable growth towards the EuroQCI, promote the development of hardware and software for quantum communications and processing, and provide training for innovation and enterprise.
On the other hand, the Ministry of Defence published its Defence Technology and Innovation Strategy in 202042 to ensure operational superiority and industrial sovereignty in a competitive global environment. This document includes quantum technology within the strategic R&D&I lines for defence, with a focus on its potential for cybersecurity, advanced data processing and secure communications. The National Institute of Aerospace Technology (INTA), under the Ministry of Defence, has positioned itself as an international benchmark in the field of space optics and is a key player in the development of quantum communications space systems, leading initiatives ranging from research to deployment. INTA has consolidated knowledge in the development of space platforms, environmental testing for space systems and a strategic network of ground stations. In addition, its capabilities in unmanned aerial systems (UAVs) reinforce its role in the development of dual-application quantum technologies.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: # 75 million

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## invested in the Complementary Quantum Communication Plan

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
The Ministry for Digital Transformation and Public Administration (MTDFP) has promoted the 2020 National Artificial Intelligence Strategy (ENIA)43 and its revision (2024)44 as a basis for possible future synergies, strengthening Spain's position in the global technological ecosystem. In the field of quantum technologies, the MTDFP has made the following investments, with funds from the Recovery Plan:
- The Quantum Spain project, coordinated by the BSC-CNS, has been launched with an investment of €22 million45, enabling the acquisition of superconducting-based quantum computing capabilities that will support research46 and innovation in the Spanish quantum ecosystem47.
- A second quantum computer has been launched, also located at the BSC-CNS. In this case, based on analogue technology, quantum annsarsr, co-financed by the EuroHPC JU with a total of €8.5 million, which allows for diversification in the construction of its quantum computing infrastructures to serve the research and innovation ecosystem.
- In order to diversify investment in quantum computing enabling technologies, a simulator and computer based on Rydberg atoms is being built through the Centre for Research in Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology (CINN)49 , located in Asturias, with €7 million in funding.
These quantum computing initiatives focus on specific projects, recognising the need for a European scale to compete in the international race. However, they are essential for creating the necessary infrastructure, the quantum ecosystem and its auxiliary industry, testing technological advances and promoting their implementation.
All these investments in infrastructure and research have generated new opportunities for innovation and application of these technologies in strategic sectors, such as telecommunications, energy and defence, fostering the development of high-impact companies and spin-offs in quantum technologies, some of which are very promising in their respective fields of activity.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## USE CASE

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Nitrogen fixation for sustainable agriculture (Nitrogen fixation)

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
The agricultural sector is seeking more sustainable solutions for fertiliser production, and quantum computing can play a key role in this process. Through advanced simulations, it is possible to design catalysts that mimic biological nitrogen fixation, reducing dependence on highly polluting industrial processes.
In addition to optimising production, this facilitates access to fertilisers for small farmers and contributes to the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions.
Spain already has experience helping spin-offs enter the market, quantum start-ups obtain funding, and demonstrating through use cases how quantum technologies can help achieve a more productive and efficient economy. A report50published in 2022 identified 32 Spanish companies interested in quantum technologies (12 of them large companies and 20 SMEs or startups), but this ecosystem has clearly grown in recent years thanks to spin-offs from research institutions. Furthermore, there is institutional consensus on the importance of promoting these technologies, aligning with the European Union in the pursuit of greater competitiveness, autonomy and technological sovereignty.
The transfer of knowledge between research and the market is therefore essential, and many of the activities are included in the PEICTI 2024-2027 Transfer and Collaboration Programme. The Centre for Technological Development and Innovation (CDTI)51has played a key role as a funding agent for quantum innovation. Over the last five years (2020-2025), €46.6 million has been allocated to a total of 40 projects focused on the development of quantum technologies and their application in sectors
diverse (mainly ICT and energy, but also with representation in the aerospace sector, industrial sectors, and security and defence). Both loans, through the Cervera Technology Transfer R&D projects52 , and grants, through the Science and Innovation Missions programme 53 , have been used.The latter is the CDTI's major commitment to supporting strategic, R&D-intensive initiatives developed in public-private partnerships. Under this typology, €21 million has already been allocated to the development of quantum technologies. These include, for example, a quantum computing and AI project to revolutionise excellence and digitalisation in the health sector through the PERTE de Salud de Vanguardia (Cutting-Edge Health PERTE).
Similarly, the participation of technology-based companies focused on developing these technologies in the NEOTEC subsidy programme54is highly representative. In total, from 2020 to date, 16 projects related to quantum technologies have been supported, with an associated subsidy of close to €5 million. For its part, within the missions of PERTE Chip55, two major projects (10 operations) have been supported with a grant of close to €4.5 million for microelectronics and semiconductors.
Quantum technology clusters have been established in various autonomous communities and local authorities in Spain to strengthen their innovation ecosystems and position themselves in this emerging field. Catalonia and the Basque Country have made significant progress in structuring their quantum ecosystems. Both autonomous communities have developed coordinated organisational models that act as points of contact and collaboration platforms for industry, academia and the public sector.
In Catalonia, the creation of the Consell de les Tecnologies Quantes de Catalunya (Quantum Technologies Council of Catalonia) was approved in 2021, with the function of advising the Government of Catalonia on policies aimed at promoting quantum technologies.
quantum. Through initiatives such as the R&D&I programme within the framework of the Complementary Plan in the area of quantum communication, Vall Mediterroney de la Ciència i les Tecnologies Quántiques56 and QuantumCAT, specific programmes have been implemented for the development of talent in this field, demonstrating a comprehensive approach to its quantum strategy, announced in 2023.
The Basque Quantum Strategy (BasQ) to promote quantum technologies, led by the Basque Government in coordination with the provincial councils of Álava, Bizkaia and Gipuzkoa, was launched in 2023. BasQ's fundamental objective is to develop the Basque Country into one of the world's leading quantum hubs, building on its scientific system specialising in quantum technologies. This strategy includes initiatives such as expanding scientific capacity, coordinating and strengthening university offerings, initiating a process to accelerate use cases aimed at companies and economic agents, and promoting new companies.
BasQ plans to install and launch a large modular quantum computing infrastructure in Donostia/San Sebastián by the end of 2025 to move towards a high-performance quantum infrastructure. From the perspective of accelerating use, this initiative complements the vision of promoting the development of European quantum computing technological sovereignty to explore solutions to new problems as new capabilities are developed57 . In addition, Bizkaia has presented its quantum industrial strategy called Biqain58 , with the aim of consolidating itself as a benchmark in quantum technologies at European and international level.
Galicia is also investing in quantum computing, positioning itself as a key player on the national quantum capabilities map. Among its initiatives, the launch of the Qmio quantum computer at the Galician Supercomputing Centre (CES-GA) stands out. This advanced infrastructure is part of the Galician Quantum Technologies Hub59 , an initiative that seeks to generate an ecosystem of innovation, applied research and technology transfer around quantum technologies.
The Community of Madrid is positioning itself in the European quantum future, with a strategy aimed at turning the region into a benchmark hub for applied quantum technologies. In addition to the deployment of MadQCI60 —a quantum cybersecurity network that will connect the metropolitan area with future European and extra-Community infrastructure— the region is promoting the creation of a quantum cluster that will facilitate companies' access to international networks, their participation in strategic R&D&I programmes, and their integration into emerging markets. This commitment is built on a solid scientific foundation, an experimental network that is already operational—the largest in Europe for quantum key distribution—and a clear vision: to consolidate a regional ecosystem that integrates the entire quantum value chain, from knowledge to business, promoting entrepreneurship, public-private collaboration, and technological autonomy.
Through the COMPUTAEX Foundation (Computing and Advanced Technologies of Extremadura), Extremadura has positioned itself as a key player in the national quantum technology ecosystem, hosting one of the quantum emulators available in Spain on classical supercomputing architectures. This resource allows the research community to access quantum capabilities and simulators, thus facilitating the development and validation of quantum algorithms in an accessible, high-performance environment.
The Regional Government of Andalusia, through the Andalusian Digital Agency (ADA) and within the framework of the Hercules project61, is funding the development of an innovation ecosystem in emerging technologies, including quantum computing. The initiative includes the integration of quantum emulation software on the Hercules supercomputer, the training of specialised personnel, and the creation of an open infrastructure so that start-ups, companies and research personnel can remotely access and work on the design and testing of quantum algorithms.
USE CASE

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text: ## Drug discovery through quantum simulations (Drug discovery through quantum simulations)

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Biomedical research benefits greatly from quantum computing's ability to model molecular interactions with extremely high precision. This makes it possible to identify promising compounds, optimise their design and reduce the time and cost of developing new drugs.
Its application is key to accelerating the discovery of treatments for complex diseases, improving the efficiency of the pharmaceutical industry.
The Autonomous Community of Aragon has also begun to develop relevant capabilities in the field of quantum technologies, highlighting the role of the Aragon Supercomputing Centre (CESAR). These initiatives are articulated within the framework of the Aragon Smart Specialisation Strategy (S3 2021-2027), which identifies quantum technologies as an emerging line of regional interest in the field of enabling technologies.

doc_id: ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
ESP_2025_Strategy of Quantum Technologies of Spain
text:
Spanish companies interested in quantum technologies in 2022
